---
title: "AI Tools for Data Science and Statistics"
subtitle: "Tools and Workflow"
author: "Erik Westlund"
date: "March 5, 2026"
bibliography: references.bib
format:
  revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    transition: slide
    background-transition: fade
    self-contained: true
    css: slides.css
---

```{r}
#| include: false
if (requireNamespace("framework", quietly = TRUE)) {
  framework::scaffold()
} else {
  if (file.exists("../scaffold.R")) source("../scaffold.R")
}
```

## {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
Session 3: Tools and Workflow
:::

## Review

We ran the same task across multiple harnesses and models.

- Same model, different harness, different outputs
- Most variation appeared in diagnostics depth, visualization quality, and limitations reporting

## GUI (vs. CLI) Agent Example (VS Code)

- Quick walkthrough of VS Code Copilot agent mode
- Same agent loop (read, edit, run, fix) but driven through the editor GUI

## Requirements for Biostatistical Work

For biostat work, we need a repeatable workflow that is:

- Safe for PHI constraints
- Reproducible with audit trails

## Git Makes It Easy To Switch Environments

Simple pattern for restricted workflows:

1. Work locally on synthetic/public data
2. Commit and push code
3. Pull code in restricted environment
4. Run on real data inside restricted environment

Code can move across environments and into models; sensitive data cannot.

## `.gitignore`

1. **Data safety:** prevent accidental commits of data, secrets, and logs
2. **Agent hygiene:** reduce irrelevant directories so agents search less noise

```
data/
*.csv
.env
.Renviron
.claude/
.cursor/
```

Agents can traverse files aggressively, so `.gitignore` can reduce noise but cannot enforce access control. Different agents/harnesses will respect `.gitignore` differently.

## What Is tmux?

`tmux` is a terminal multiplexer: it lets you run and manage multiple terminal workflows inside one persistent session.

- **Session:** named workspace for a project
- **Window:** tab inside a session (agent, git, REPL)
- **Pane:** split view inside a window

Why it's useful:

- Persist work when local terminals close or SSH disconnects
- Run multiple agents and tools in parallel layouts

## tmux Workflow Pattern

Use one launcher script per project to bootstrap a consistent session layout, plus lightweight aliases:

- `tm<project>`: runs your project launcher alias (example: `tmstats`)
- `tma <session>`: attach to an existing session (example: `tma biostat`)
- `tmk <session>`: kill an existing session (example: `tmk biostat`)

This keeps daily startup repeatable across local and remote environments. See the tmux example.

## Use Agents to Learn These Tools

- Have agents write your tmux launcher scripts; this is high ROI to use, low ROI to learn
- Agents are very good at git and shell tooling, use them for advice
- Use agents to teach you *why* they do what they do
- Studying what LLMs is a great way to learn some tricks of the trade

## Context Files: What to Standardize

Use concise, durable rules in context files:

| File | Read by | Scope |
|:-----|:--------|:------|
| `CLAUDE.md` | Claude Code, OpenCode | Per-directory, stacks |
| `AGENTS.md` | Codex, OpenCode, emerging standard | Per-directory |
| `.cursorrules` | Cursor | Project root |

Many modern agents already know to look for common context files.

## Context Scope and Stacking

- Start directory controls which context files are in scope
- Context can stack from nested paths
- More context increases token/time cost

Agents can often infer a lot by reading project files directly, so context files should constrain behavior, not duplicate documentation.

## Context Files: Evidence and Balance

An ETH Zurich study found that AI-generated `AGENTS.md` files reduced agent success rates and increased cost on SWE-bench tasks [@gloaguen2026agentsmd]. However, hand-written context files performed better.

- Write your own context files
- Keep context files minimal and high-signal
- Even if there's a "SWE-bench" penalty, these files are still useful to guide agents on project-specific logic, compliance rules, and hard constraints
- Avoid large auto-generated boilerplate blocks

## Warning

Just because a context file says not to do something doesn't mean the agen't wont do it.

## MCP: Model Context Protocol

Model Context Protocol (MCP) is a standard way to connect agents to tools, data sources, and services.

- Gives agents a structured interface instead of ad hoc shell/API glue
- Supports discoverable tool definitions, inputs, and outputs
- Helps teams govern how agent access is exposed

## MCP Goals

- **Control:** approved systems can be exposed intentionally, not implicitly
- **Safety:** permissioned connectors can limit what an agent can reach
- **Portability:** the same MCP tool can be reused across different agent harnesses

For this course context, MCP is useful when code can move but sensitive data cannot.

## MCP Example: EHR Query Gateway

One practical use case is controlled cohort discovery from EHR systems.

- Agent calls an MCP tool like `ehr_cohort_counts`
- Tool accepts structured inputs (ICD codes, date window, age filters, site)
- MCP server runs approved parameterized queries inside the restricted network
- Tool returns aggregates or summary tables, not row-level PHI

Result: the analyst can iterate quickly on feasibility questions while keeping direct database access tightly governed.

## MCP Adoption Has Mixed Momentum

MCP remains valuable for governed access, but some teams report overhead outweighs benefits for routine coding tasks.

- **Overhead:** tool schemas and capability manifests can add context bloat
- **Latency/cost:** repeated tool negotiation and calls can increase token/time cost
- **Redundancy:** many modern agents can use native tools directly (for example `psql`) when schema/context are clear

Practical takeaway: use MCP where governance and boundary control are essential; use simpler direct tool paths where they are sufficient and safe.

## Skills 

Skills are reusable, task-specific instruction bundles for agents.

- Package best-practice prompts and constraints in one place
- Reduce per-run setup overhead for recurring tasks
- Improve consistency across users and sessions

## Anatomy of a Skill

Reference: [anthropics/skills](https://github.com/anthropics/skills)

Typical structure:

- `SKILL.md` with frontmatter (`name`, `description`) plus operating instructions
- Optional `scripts/` for reusable helper commands
- Optional `examples/` and reference resources (templates, assets, docs)
- Optional `LICENSE.txt` when sharing skill code/content

Minimal template from the repo:

```markdown
---
name: my-skill-name
description: what this skill does and when to use it
---
```

It's just text.

## Skills: Caution

- Agents are hit-or-miss for when they invoke skills
- Skills have not been studied, so like with AGENTS/CLAUDE.md files, use caution
- Consider the token cost -- these skills will be sent along with your prompts when invoked

## Context File Anatomy (Portable Pattern)

Adapted from the Laravel Skills docs structure:

- **Context:** scope, assumptions, and environment boundaries
- **Rules:** explicit requirements the agent should follow
- **Examples:** concrete snippets showing correct behavior
- **Anti-patterns:** known failure modes to avoid
- **References:** source docs and internal standards

Reference: [skills.laravel.cloud/docs](https://skills.laravel.cloud/docs)

## Example: ggplot2 Style Context File

```markdown
## Context
- All plots use a shared ggplot2 theme for publication.

## Rules
- Use `theme_minimal(base_size = 12)` as the base theme.
- Color palettes: `scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2")` for categorical,
  `scale_fill_viridis_c()` for continuous.
- Always label axes with units: `labs(x = "Age (years)")`.
- Add `caption = "Source: NHANES 2017–2020"` (or relevant source).

## Examples
- `geom_point(alpha = 0.6, size = 2)` for scatter plots.
- `geom_bar(stat = "identity") + coord_flip()` for ranked bars.
- Facets: `facet_wrap(~ group, scales = "free_y")`.

## Anti-patterns
- Rainbow color scales or default R palette.
- Unlabeled axes or missing units.
- Dual y-axes (use facets instead).

## References
- Project style guide in `docs/plot-standards.md`
```

## Context Anti-Patterns to Avoid

- Rule lists that are vague ("be rigorous", "use best practices")
- Conflicting constraints across multiple context files
- Large style boilerplate that increases token cost without guidance value
- Missing references that force the agent to guess standards

## {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
Workflow Tips
:::

## Give the Agent a Place to Write

- Keep a `docs/` or `notes/` directory for plans, prompts, and analysis notes
- Tell the agent to write there: summaries, decision logs, intermediate findings
- Creates a paper trail you can review, share, or pick up later
- Also useful as context for future sessions: the agent can read its own prior notes

## Practical Workflow Tips

- **Checkpoint with commits**: commit after each meaningful step so you have rollback points when the agent goes sideways
- **Ask for explanation**: tell the agent to explain its reasoning, not just produce code; catches misunderstandings before they compound
- **Play agents against each other**: use multiple agents with different strengths to review each other's outputs and catch errors

## {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
Security & Risks
:::

## Prompt Injection & Data Risks

- **Prompt injection**: malicious instructions hidden in data, web pages, or tool outputs can hijack agent behavior
- **Data exfiltration**: agents with tool access (MCP, shell) can be tricked into sending data to external services
- **Context poisoning**: untrusted content in context files or pasted text can alter agent behavior silently
- Mitigation: review agent actions, limit tool permissions, don't paste untrusted content

## Practical Security Habits

- **Never feed PHI/PII to cloud-hosted models**: use local/institutionally-hosted models or simulated data for sensitive work
- **Review before you run**: read generated shell commands and API calls before approving; agents can hallucinate destructive operations
- **Scope tool access**: give agents the minimum permissions they need; avoid "yolo" mode on sensitive projects
- **Treat agent output as untrusted**: validate results the same way you'd review a colleague's code

## {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
Demo Sequence
:::

## Demo 1: Git Push/Pull Workflow (`demos/5-git-push-pull`)

- Work locally on synthetic/public data with agents
- Commit and push code; pull into restricted environment
- Restricted side: run on real data, inspect schema and aggregates only
- Emphasize audit trail and boundary discipline throughout

## Demo 2: tmux for Agent Workflows (`demos/6-tmux-workflow`)

- Show session/window/pane structure
- Show launcher script pattern
- Show attach/kill alias flow (`tma`, `tmk`)

## Demo 3: Two-Machine PHI Workflow (`demos/7-phi-two-machine`)

- Synthetic/simulated development on unrestricted machine
- Push code to restricted machine for real data runs
- Agents never touch PHI directly

## Demo 4: Context A/B Exercise (`demos/8-context-comparison`)

- Palmer Penguins scatter plot: bill length vs body mass by species
- Same notebook and prompt in both directories
- `with-context/` has an `AGENTS.md` specifying theme, colors, axis labels, captions
- `without-context/` has no style guidance

Run an agent in each directory and compare the output styling.
